metric_value: Half-million Federal Reserve employees who touched paper nightly replaced by electronic clearing settling in seconds.
notes: Kastner's opening declaration that the automation problem he identified at age 12 has been solved.

metric_value: Airlines no longer print physical tickets.
notes: Cited as evidence that labor-intensive commerce has been essentially eliminated.

metric_value: Restaurant reconciliation runs on a tablet.
notes: Third example of labor-intensive commerce replaced by automation in a single working lifetime.

metric_value: The deeper lesson of fifty years is not what machines accomplished but what remained stubbornly, persistently, irreducibly human.
notes: Central analytical conclusion of the entire volume.

metric_value: Genuine technical breakthrough followed by organizational resistance, economic friction, and human misalignment making adoption slower, messier, more expensive than engineers predicted.
notes: Kastner's generalized framework applied across timesharing, fault tolerance, relational databases, client-server, and the web.

metric_value: Mainframe fell not because minicomputer engineering was better but because economics inverted and glass-house culture could not adapt quickly enough.
notes: Kastner's retrospective analytical judgment on the mainframe-to-minicomputer transition.

metric_name: Why proprietary minicomputer fell to Unix
metric_value: Margin compression was mathematically inevitable; no vendor found a durable strategy against commodity hardware. Open standards obvious to many by 1985.
notes: Kastner's retrospective on the Unix open-systems transition.

metric_value: Fault-tolerant niche defended not by patents but by difficulty of making 'uptime' viscerally real to a buying committee and by the 'pull the board' demonstration.
notes: Key insight about how Stratus sustained competitive advantage through buyer psychology rather than IP.

metric_name: Cloud/SaaS/AI will follow historical adoption pattern
metric_value: Cloud, SaaS, and AI are genuine transitions that will encounter same organizational inertia, misaligned incentives, gap between architecture and enterprise willingness to reorganize.
notes: Kastner explicitly extends his historical pattern to present-day technology transitions.

metric_value: The infrastructure will move faster than the institution, as it always has.
notes: Concise formulation of Kastner's core thesis on technology adoption lag.

metric_value: Prescient on: open systems transition, client-server persistence, relational database dominance, analytics democratization, integration complexity acceleration.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Kastner's self-assessment of Aberdeen's analytical track record.

metric_value: The web moved faster than any analyst predicted.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Explicit acknowledgment of a timing miss by Aberdeen on web adoption speed.

metric_value: Predicted Unix would standardize cleanly; instead Linux won on licensing, not elegance.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Honest acknowledgment of a specific analytical miss regarding the Unix/Linux outcome.

metric_value: Linux won on licensing, not elegance—contrary to Aberdeen's prediction of clean Unix standardization.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Distinctive analytical observation about what actually drove the Linux outcome.

metric_value: Predicted data analytics would become a strategic capability—correct, but later than forecast; organizational resistance to cross-functional data integration deeper than models captured.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Identifies the gap between correct directional forecast and underestimated human/cultural resistance.

metric_name: Largest analytical miss was cultural, not technical
metric_value: Correctly forecast technology transitions; largely misforecast human ones. Organizational resistance to data integration was deeper and more durable than frameworks captured.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Kastner's most self-critical analytical admission.

metric_name: Aberdeen cooperative model first seven years
metric_value: Built on cooperative model that aligned incentives, rewarded depth, and produced genuinely differentiated work.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Kastner's description of the partnership-era Aberdeen culture.

metric_name: Published research as marketing asset insight
metric_value: Correctly understood published research was a marketing asset, not just a product—anticipated content marketing by fifteen years.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Kastner claims Aberdeen anticipated content marketing by ~15 years, placing this insight circa early 1990s.

metric_value: Technical credibility opened doors that market analysis could not.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Kastner's articulation of Aberdeen's competitive differentiation strategy.

metric_value: Venture-backed Aberdeen was a different firm from partnership-era Aberdeen—more capable in some dimensions, less distinctive in most that had mattered.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Generalized observation about how growth capital transforms firm culture and identity.

metric_value: The Harte-Hanks acquisition created wealth and destroyed culture, more or less simultaneously.
source_page: What I Got Right, and What I Missed
notes: Kastner's blunt summary of the go-go years acquisition. Most honest summary of the period.

metric_name: IBM System/360 Model 65 CPU time cost
metric_value: $360 per hour in 1969.
notes: Kastner's personal data point anchoring the compute scarcity era.

metric_value: $7,200 per year.
notes: Used to illustrate that in 1969 the machine was the scarce resource and human labor was cheap.

metric_value: CPU time at $360/hr vs. annual salary $7,200: machine was scarce, human labor was cheap. Every saved microsecond was economic conservation.
notes: Core quantitative example of the pre-inversion era economic logic.

metric_value: By leaving Aberdeen in 2008, inversion nearly complete: compute abundant; scarce resource was judgment—understanding what technology does in the hands of a real organization.
notes: Kastner dates the near-completion of the scarcity inversion to circa 2008.

metric_value: AI inference on commodity hardware; foundation models available to any developer with a credit card. Constraint is not whether machine can do the thing but whether organization can govern it.
notes: Kastner's 2026 assessment of where the scarcity inversion stands.

metric_name: The constraint is organizational, not computational
metric_value: Constraint is whether human organization can decide what to do, govern how to do it, and absorb consequences at scale—not whether machine can perform the task.
notes: Kastner's 2026 restatement of his core thesis as applied to AI.

metric_name: Cape Cod restaurant problem as originating insight
metric_value: Restaurant reconciliation problem encountered at Cape Cod in 1960 is the same organizational constraint seen at Chase Manhattan, Stratus, DEC, and Aberdeen.
notes: Kastner links his earliest computing observation to his final synthesis, closing the memoir's conceptual arc.

metric_name: Chase Manhattan as example of organizational technology friction
metric_value: Chase Manhattan cited alongside Stratus, DEC, and Aberdeen as institutions where technology changes but organizational physics does not.
notes: Named as part of Kastner's universal pattern across his career.

metric_name: Technology is only part of the system
metric_value: To live inside the machine for fifty years is to learn that technology is only one part of the system. The rest is economics, institutions, incentives, and human nature.
notes: Kastner's final summative statement; the closing thesis of the memoir.

metric_name: GitHub archive of Aberdeen primary materials
metric_value: Archive of primary materials from career and Aberdeen years available at GitHub: github.com/shorttack/aberdeen-group-archive/tree/main
notes: Kastner notes the existence of a publicly accessible archive of primary source materials.

metric_value: By any reasonable measure, we delivered. Went looking for automation at twelve years old, spent fifty years helping build it.
notes: Kastner's affirmative verdict on whether the technical mission of his career was accomplished.

metric_value: Open standards were obvious to many analysts by 1985, yet proprietary minicomputer to Unix transition took longer due to commodity hardware margin compression.
notes: Kastner's dating of when the open standards outcome was already intellectually clear.

metric_value: Left Aberdeen in 2008.
notes: Date of Kastner's departure from Aberdeen used as temporal anchor for the compute inversion timeline.

metric_name: Kastner as programmer conserving machine resources
metric_value: Every microsecond saved, every instruction eliminated, every core dump decoded was an act of economic conservation in 1969.
notes: Vivid description of the economic logic of programming when compute was the scarce resource.

metric_name: Physics of organizations does not change
metric_value: The technology changes. The physics of organizations does not.
notes: Kastner's most aphoristic statement of his central thesis.

metric_name: SaaS as genuine transition subject to institutional friction
metric_value: SaaS is a genuine technical transition that will encounter organizational inertia and misaligned incentives, same as prior transitions.
notes: Kastner applies his historical pattern explicitly to SaaS.

metric_name: Kastner's age at first automation interest
metric_value: Went looking for automation at twelve years old (circa 1960).
notes: Establishes the starting point of Kastner's fifty-year computing career arc.

metric_value: See companion study kastner-technology-breadth-memoir-2026 for quantified 47-year coverage breadth (2,537 distinct technologies, 14 thematic rollups).
